full_response:
Let  

v₁ = 0.90 c  (outbound speed) ,  γ₁ = 1/√(1–v₁²/c²) = 1/√(1–0.81) = 2.294  
v₂ = 0.99 c  (inbound speed) ,  γ₂ = 1/√(1–v₂²/c²) = 1/√(1–0.9801) = 7.089  

Assumptions  
1. Earth and the far–away planet are at rest with respect to one another (same inertial frame).  
2. “1 year on the planet’’ is proper time for the ship (it is landed and at rest there) and equals 1 yr in the Earth/planet frame.  
3. Accelerations are instantaneous, so each leg of the trip is purely inertial with the stated speeds.

Notation  
t₁ , t₂  = Earth-frame durations of the outbound and inbound trips.  
τ₁ , τ₂  = the ship’s proper times for those two legs.  

Time dilation on each leg  

τ₁ = t₁ / γ₁ ,  τ₂ = t₂ / γ₂.  (1)

Distance to the planet  

Let the Earth-frame distance be d.  
t₁ = d / v₁ ,  t₂ = d / v₂.  (2)

Total proper time recorded by the ship  

τ_total = τ₁ + 1 yr (stay) + τ₂ = 5 yr  ⇒ τ₁ + τ₂ = 4 yr.  (3)

Substitute (1) and (2) into (3):

d /(v₁ γ₁) + d /(v₂ γ₂) = 4.                             (4)

Define  

B₁ ≡ 1 /(v₁ γ₁) = 1 /(0.90·2.294) = 0.4842,  
B₂ ≡ 1 /(v₂ γ₂) = 1 /(0.99·7.089) = 0.1425.

Then A ≡ B₁ + B₂ = 0.6267, and eq. (4) gives d = 4/A.

Outbound proper time  

τ₁ = d /(v₁ γ₁) = (4/A)·B₁ = 4 B₁ /(B₁ + B₂)
     = 4·0.4842 / 0.6267
     ≃ 3.09 yr.

(Inbound proper time τ₂ = 4 yr – τ₁ ≈ 0.91 yr, a useful check.)

\boxed{\tau_{\text{outbound}} \;\approx\; 3.1\ \text{years}}

Confidence: 88%
